content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Rebecca Moon 
Sr. Assistant City Attorney 
456 W. Olive Avenue 
P.O. Box 3707 
Sunnyvale, CA 94088 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-074 
Dear Ms. Moon: 
May 17, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Sunnyvale Planning 
Commissioners Russell Melton and Sue Harrison regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTIONS 
Does the Act prohibit Commissioners Melton or Harrison from ta1cing part in governmental 
decisions relating to the Butcher's Comer Project? 
CONCLUSIONS 
No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioners Melton or Harrison from taking part in 
decisions relating to the Project. 
FACTS 
You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Sunnyvale and the authorized 
representative of Sunnyvale Planning Commissioners Russell Melton and Sue Harrison. The City's 
Planning Commission will soon consider decisions relating to the Butcher's Comer Project, a 
. mixed-use development on a currently undeveloped 5.1-acre site located at 871 East Fremont 
Avenue in the City. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-074 
Page No. 2 
The Project is comprised of a total of 153 residential units, including 114 apartment units 
within two multi-story buildings and 39 two-story townhome units. The Project also includes 6,936 
square feet of non-residential ( office or retail) use within the first floor of the apartment building 
fronting El Camino Real. 
The Project site is at the intersection of El Camino Real and Wolfe Road, two of the City's 
major thoroughfares. The surrounding area is generally characterized by wide streets with high 
traffic volumes, multi-family residential housing developments, commercial or retail businesses, 
and medical offices. Nearby land uses include gas stations, small strip malls, and a miniature golf 
course. The Project site is immediately adjacent to a two-story medical office building and two- and 
three-story multi-family housing developments. Lower density single-family residential 
neighborhoods are located west and southeast of the Project site. The development of the Project 
site is anticipated in the City's General Plan. 
With respect to the Project's impact on traffic, the Draft Environmental Impact Report 
("DEIR") for the Project, released on April 8, 2016, estimates that the Project will generate 1,182 
net new trips on a typical weekday. Of the external trips, 79 net trips will occur during the weekday 
morning peak hour and 108 trips during the evening peak hour. An estimated 48 percent of the new 
trips will be on East Fremont Avenue, 23 percent on El Camino Real, and 9 percent on Wolfe Road 
south of El Camino Real. However, the DEIR provides that additional vehicle trips generated by the 
Project are not expected to substantially increase cut-through traffic, vehicle queues, or delay. 
The DEIR identifies a significant and unavoidable traffic impact at the intersection of East 
Fremont Avenue and Kingfisher Way. However, this intersection is located west of the Project site, 
farther removed from either of the Commissioner's residence than the Project itself. And either 
Commissioner has other, more convenient options to access East Fremont A venue or El Camino 
Real in closer proximity to each's residence. 
The Project will increase traffic congestion at certain times at the intersections of El Camino 
Real, Wolfe Road, and East Fremont Avenue, where traffic volumes are already heavy during peak 
hours. However, according to the DEIR, intersections other than East Fremont Avenue and 
Kingfisher Way will continue to operate at acceptable levels. 
The DEIR identifies thirteen other development projects have been approved and are in 
construction or awaiting construction in the City of Sunnyvale or nearby in the City of Cupertino 
that will also contribute to traffic congestion in the area surrounding the Project site, including the 
Apple Campus 2, the future headquarters of Apple Inc., less than 4 miles south of the Project site in 
the City of Cupertino. 
The DEIR also identifies significant impacts from the Project in the following areas: air 
quality, biological resources (tree removal and disturbance of nesting birds and bats), cultural 
resources, and noise. However, the DEIR concludes that these impacts can be reduced to a less than 
significant level with appropriate mitigation measures. 
Commissioner Melton owns a single-family residence approximately 2,372 feet south of the 
Project site, three large residential blocks south of the Project site and one block east of Wolfe 
Road. Commissioner Harrison owns a single-family residence approximately 1,267 feet southeast 


File No. A-16-074 
Page No. 3 
of the Project site, two large residential blocks east of the Project site and two blocks south of El 
Camino Real. The Commissioners' respective residences are located within an existing, built-out 
single-family residential neighborhood, on residential streets with relatively low traffic volumes and 
speeds. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87 l 00 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b ), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. 
With respect to the decisions at issue, Commissioners Melton and Harrison each have a 
financial interest in real property in their respective residences under the Act. Therefore, we must 
determine whether the financial effect on these interests is both foreseeable and material. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision, and an interest is explicitly involved in the decision if the interest is a 
named party, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Neither Commissioner's residence 
is "explicitly involved" in the decisions relating to the Project. Therefore, the effect of those 
decisions on either Commissioner Melton or Commissioner Harrison's respective residences is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial interest in 
real property is material. Relevant to your inquiry and the facts presented, the financial effect on an 
official's real property interest in his residence is material if the decision: 
• Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by substantially altering 
traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the 
official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including 
odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property 
parcel in which the official has a financial interest (Regulation l 8702.2(a)(10)); or 
• Would cause a reasonably prudent person using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's 
property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(12).) 


Traffic Levels 
File No. A-16-074 
Page No. 4 
We first consider whether the decision would change the character of either Commissioner's 
residence by substantially altering the traffic levels of properties surrounding either residence. 
According to the DElR, the project will generate 1,182 net new trips on a typical weekday. 
An estimated 48 percent of new trips will be on East Fremont Avenue, 23 percent on El Camino 
Real, and 9 percent on Wolfe Road south of El Camino Real. However, the DEIR provides that 
additional vehicle trips generated by the Project are not expected to substantially increase cut­
through traffic, vehicle queues, or delay. 
The DEIR identifies a significant and unavoidable traffic impact at the intersection of East 
Fremont Avenue and Kingfisher Way. However, this intersection is located west of the Project site, 
farther removed from either Commissioner's residence than the Project itself. Either Commissioner 
has other, more convenient options to access East Fremont A venue or El Camino Real in closer 
proximity to each's residence. And the facts presented do not provide any indication that either 
Commissioner regularly uses this intersection. 
The Project will slightly worsen traffic operations at certain times at the intersections of El 
Camino Real, Wolfe Road, and East Fremont Avenue, where traffic volumes are already heavy 
during peak hours. However, according to the DEIR, intersections other than East Fremont Avenue 
and Kingfisher Way will continue to operate at acceptable levels. 
Commissioner Melton's residence is three large residential blocks south of the Project site 
and one block east of Wolfe Road, approximately 2,372 feet from the Project site. Commissioner 
Harrison's residence is two large residential blocks east of the Project site and two blocks south of 
El Camino Real, approximately 1,267 feet southeast of the Project site. The Commissioners' 
respective residences are located within an existing, built-out single-family residential 
neighborhood, on residential streets with relatively low traffic volumes and speeds. Although the 
Project would add to traffic congestion in the general area surrounding the Project site, the facts 
presented do not provide any evidence that the Project would substantially alter traffic levels on the 
residential streets on which the Commissioners' respective residences are located. 
Therefore, we find that the Project would not change the character of either Commissioner's 
residence by substantially altering the traffic levels of properties surrounding either residence. 
Intensity of Use 
We next consider whether the decision would change the character of either 
Commissioner's residence by substantially altering the intensity of use of properties surrounding 
either residence. 
Although the Project site is currently undeveloped, the area immediately surrounding it is 
highly developed, includes multi-family residential housing developments, commercial or retail 
businesses, and medical offices, and has wide streets with high traffic volumes. Nearby land uses 
include gas stations, small strip malls, and a miniature golf course. The Project site is immediately 


File No. A-16-074 
Page No. 5 
adjacent to a two-story medical office building and two- and three-story multifamily housing 
developments. The development of the Project site is anticipated in the City's General Plan. 
The DEIR identifies significant impacts from the Project in the following areas: air quality, 
biological resources (tree removal and disturbance of nesting birds and bats), cultural resources, and 
noise. However, the DEIR indicates that these impacts can be reduced to a less than significant 
level with appropriate Įitigation measures. 
Lower density single-family residential neighborhoods are located west and southeast of the 
Project site, and the Commissioners' respective residences are both located in the single-family 
residential neighborhood southeast of the Project site. You state that it does not appear that the 
Project will have any impact on either Commissioner's parking, views, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality at their respective residences. And the facts presented do not provide any evidence that the 
intensity of use of properties in the Commissioners' single-family residential neighborhood will be 
altered by the decisions. 
Therefore, we find that the Project would not change the character of either Commissioner's 
residence by substantially altering the intensity of use of properties surrounding either residence. 
Affect or Influence the Market Value of Either Commissioner's Residence? 
Finally, we consider whether the decisions relating to the Project would cause a reasonably 
prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the 
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market 
value of either Commissioner's residence. 
The Project is a large mixed-use development on a currently undeveloped site in a highly 
developed area that includes single-family and multi-family residential neighborhoods as well as 
the busy commercial and retail corridor on El Camino Real. However, the Project would not change 
the character of either Commissioner's residence by substantially altering the traffic levels or 
intensity of use of properties surrounding either residence. 
While the Project will generally result in increased traffic congestion on the major 
thoroughfares in the vicinity of the Project, traffic volumes on these roads are already heavy at 
certain times of day. Furthermore, the DEIR indicates that traffic volumes on these roads are 
expected to worsen due to thirteen other approved projects in construction or awaiting construction 
in the area, including the Apple Campus 2, the future headquarters of Apple Inc., less than 4 miles 
south of the Project site in the City of Cupertino. Given the existing traffic congestion in the area 
surrounding the Project site, and the anticipated additional traffic congestion from the thirteen other 
approved projects in the area, the additional traffic congestion attributable to the Project will not 
result in a change in conditions that would affect the market value of either Commissioner's 
residence. 
Therefore, we find that the decisions at issue will not affect or influence the market value of 
either Commissioner's residence. 


File No. A-16-074 
Page No. 6 
Accordingly, we conclude that the decisions relating to the Project will not have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioners' real property interests in 
their respective residences, and the Act does not prohibit the Commissioners from taking part in 
those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Commissioners Melton or Harrison from ta1cing part in governmental 
decisions relating to the Butcher's Comer Project?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioners Melton or Harrison from taking part in 
decisions relating to the Project.

sections.analysis:
Section 87 l 00 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b ), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material

El Camino Real, approximately 1,267 feet southeast of the Project site. The Commissioners' 
respective residences are located within an existing, built-out single-family residential 
neighborhood, on residential streets with relatively low traffic volumes and speeds. Although the 
Project would add to traffic congestion in the general area surrounding the Project site, the facts

residence by substantially altering the traffic levels of properties surrounding either residence.

We next consider whether the decision would change the character of either 
Commissioner's residence by substantially altering the intensity of use of properties surrounding

decisions relating to the Butcher's Comer Project?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit Commissioners Melton or Harrison from taking part in 
decisions relating to the Project.
